{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}}
{{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (small)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} (candidate shrine)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown
tradition: mid-{{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|WD=Q8090|1=6th century}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Central six-shrine gongen of Mt. Kirishima |札所等 = Kirishima Rokusha Gongen |例祭 = November 18 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Miyazaki|label=Kirishimamine Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=11|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Kirishimamine Shrine''' (きりしまみねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hosono, {{ill|lt=Kobayashi|Q861283|WD=Q861283|1=Kobayashi, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|Q130300|WD=Q130300|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. It is a candidate shrine for an {{ill|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}Kobayashi City History, Vol.3, pp.1095-1097 * {{ill|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} and {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyuga mythology)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} Ninigi, Hikohohodemi, and Ugayafukiaezu are called the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|WD=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}, ancestors of the {{ill|lt=Imperial Household|Q909452|WD=Q909452|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, and the other three are their consorts. === Origin === [[Image:Kirishimamine Jinja Sando.jpg|thumb|270px|Approach]]{{出典の明記| date = 2022年2月| section = 1}} The founding date is unknown, though tradition places it around the mid-6th century. It is regarded as a shrine deeply connected with the descent of the heavenly grandson ({{ill|lt=Tenson kōrin|Q1084733|WD=Q1084733|1=Tenson kōrin}}) and the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|WD=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. According to shrine tradition, the "Kirishimamine deity" in the entry for August 1, {{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093|1=837}} ({{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 4) of the ''{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}'' - where Miyazaki's Tonō, Tsuma, Eda, and Kirishimamine deities were granted status as official shrines - refers to this shrine{{Cite book|和書 |title=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records |date=1988-09-30|year=1988|publisher=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. In ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'', it is stated that in {{ill|lt=858|Q30371|WD=Q30371|1=858}} ({{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} 2), it was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower. There is also a theory identifying this shrine with "Kirishima Shrine" in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' for Morokata District, Hyuga Province (thus a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=candidate for Engishiki shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}){{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. Originally it was enshrined at "Setao" (Semonto Hill / Setoo), between {{ill|lt=Takachiho Peak|Q3514128|WD=Q3514128|1=Mount Takachiho}} and {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|WD=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}}, in present-day {{ill|lt=Takaharu|Q1206617|WD=Q1206617|1=Takaharu, Miyazaki}}, Nishimorokata District, Miyazaki. It was therefore also called '''Setao Gongen''', and its betto temple was said to be Setao-ji. Around the Tenkei and Tenryaku eras, priest {{ill|lt=Shoku|Q11491623|WD=Q11491623|1=Shōkū}} is said to have founded four other shrines including Himamori Shrine around the mountain foothills, and together with Kirishimamine Shrine (also called the Central Gongen Shrine of Mt. Kirishima) they were called the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=shinbutsu shugo|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, {{ill|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|Q239847|WD=Q239847|1=Vairocana}} was enshrined at Setao-ji on the approach, and the shrine came to be called '''Kirishima-san Chuo Rokusho (Rokusha) Gongen''' ('''Kirishimamine Shrine'''). As the name suggests, it became the center of the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}, and Kirishima worship flourished. In {{ill|lt=1112|Q19525|WD=Q19525|1=1112}} ({{ill|lt=Tenei|Q1193915|WD=Q1193915|1=Ten'ei}} 3), eruption of {{ill|lt=Karakuni-dake|Q3321904|WD=Q3321904|1=Mount Karakuni}}, and in {{ill|lt=1167|Q19678|WD=Q19678|1=1167}} ({{ill|lt=Nin'an (Japan)|Q3577285|WD=Q3577285|lt=Nin'an|1=Nin'an}} 2), eruption of Ōhata-yama burned shrine buildings, but each time they were rebuilt on the original site. In {{ill|lt=1234|Q5424|WD=Q5424|1=1234}} ({{ill|lt=Bunryaku|Q1194125|WD=Q1194125|1=Bunryaku}} 1), a nearby eruption of {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|WD=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}} again burned the shrine, and because a spring called Ama-no-I from this highland site dried up, it was moved near the branch shrine Kiribe Ōji Shrine, and that place was called "Shin-Setao". However, in {{ill|lt=1716|Q6892|WD=Q6892|1=1716}} ({{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 1), eruption of {{ill|lt=Shinmoedake|Q514392|WD=Q514392|1=Shinmoedake}} burned it again. The sacred body was first moved to Imabo Gongen and then to Okahara in Hosono village, but as the "Shin-Setao" site became completely deserted, in {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952|1=1729}} (Kyoho 14) shrine buildings were rebuilt on the mid-slope of {{ill|lt=Hinamori-dake|Q10942052|WD=Q10942052|1=Hinamori Dake}} and remained there until early Meiji. That location is still called Tsukiji{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. It was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} in {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}} (Meiji 5), and after combining {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hinamori Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (also counted among {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}) in {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6), it was moved to the former site of Hinamori Shrine (present location), where it remains today. In ''{{ill|lt=Sangoku Meisho Zue|Q11354726|WD=Q11354726|1=Sangoku meisho zue}}'', a record of famous places in {{ill|lt=Satsuma Domain|Q676187|WD=Q676187|1=Satsuma Domain}}, it is written as "'''Kirishima Dai-gongen-gu'''". Since ancient times it flourished as a noted shrine worshipped by the clans that ruled this area, including the {{ill|lt=Shimazu clan|Q1061406|WD=Q1061406|1=Shimazu clan}}, {{ill|lt=Ito clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280|1=Itō clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Kitahara clan|Q11401254|WD=Q11401254|1=Kitahara clan}}. {{ill|lt=Shimazu Yoshihiro|Q1156288|WD=Q1156288|1=Shimazu Yoshihiro}} is also said to have worshipped here while based at {{ill|lt=Iino Castle|Q11666711|WD=Q11666711|1=Iino Castle}}, and to have donated 100 hyo of rice each year as shrine lands. === Cultural properties === * Kobayashi City designated tangible cultural property ** Six sacred statues of Kirishimamine === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * ''Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records'', Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office, 1988 * Tatsuhiko Kuroiwa, "Kirishimamine Shrine" (in Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture Editorial Committee, ''Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture'', Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, p.251) * ''Kobayashi City History Vol.3'' (Kobayashi City History Editorial Committee, 2000) === See also === * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Shrine|Q11660899|WD=Q11660899|1=Kirishima Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}} **'''Kirishimamine Shrine''' **'''Hinamori Shrine''' **{{ill|lt=Sano Shrine|Q3472357|WD=Q3472357|1=Sano Shrine}} **Higashi Kirishima Shrine **Kirishima Higashi Shrine **{{ill|lt=Kirishima Jingū|Q704622|WD=Q704622|1=Kirishima Jingū}} * {{ill|lt=Hoko-in (Kobayashi)|Q17219459|WD=Q17219459|lt=Hoko-in|1=Hoko-in}} - former betto temple before Hinamori Gongen was merged into Kirishimamine Shrine {{神道 横}} === External links === * [https://minezinzya.wixsite.com/kirishimamine Official website of Kirishimamine Shrine] {{DEFAULTSORT:きりしまみねしんしや}} {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=宮崎県}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hyūga Province]] [[Category:宮崎県の神社]] [[Category:日向国の式内社]] [[Category:山岳信仰]] [[Category:宮崎県の旧県社]] [[Category:小林市の建築物]] [[Category:小林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

== {{神社 |名称 = Kirishimamine Shrine |画像 = [[Image:Kirishimamine Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|Haiden of Kirishimamine Shrine]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|Q130300|WD=Q130300|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kobayashi|Q861283|WD=Q861283|1=Kobayashi, Miyazaki}} Hosono 4937 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|31|57|57.9|N|130|57|28.3|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}

{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}}
{{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (small)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} (candidate shrine)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown
tradition: mid-{{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|WD=Q8090|1=6th century}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Central six-shrine gongen of Mt. Kirishima |札所等 = Kirishima Rokusha Gongen |例祭 = November 18 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Miyazaki|label=Kirishimamine Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=11|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Kirishimamine Shrine''' (きりしまみねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hosono, {{ill|lt=Kobayashi|Q861283|WD=Q861283|1=Kobayashi, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|Q130300|WD=Q130300|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. It is a candidate shrine for an {{ill|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}Kobayashi City History, Vol.3, pp.1095-1097 * {{ill|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} and {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyuga mythology)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} Ninigi, Hikohohodemi, and Ugayafukiaezu are called the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|WD=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}, ancestors of the {{ill|lt=Imperial Household|Q909452|WD=Q909452|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, and the other three are their consorts. === Origin === [[Image:Kirishimamine Jinja Sando.jpg|thumb|270px|Approach]]{{出典の明記| date = 2022年2月| section = 1}} The founding date is unknown, though tradition places it around the mid-6th century. It is regarded as a shrine deeply connected with the descent of the heavenly grandson ({{ill|lt=Tenson kōrin|Q1084733|WD=Q1084733|1=Tenson kōrin}}) and the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|WD=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. According to shrine tradition, the "Kirishimamine deity" in the entry for August 1, {{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093|1=837}} ({{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 4) of the ''{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}'' - where Miyazaki's Tonō, Tsuma, Eda, and Kirishimamine deities were granted status as official shrines - refers to this shrine{{Cite book|和書 |title=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records |date=1988-09-30|year=1988|publisher=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. In ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'', it is stated that in {{ill|lt=858|Q30371|WD=Q30371|1=858}} ({{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} 2), it was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower. There is also a theory identifying this shrine with "Kirishima Shrine" in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' for Morokata District, Hyuga Province (thus a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=candidate for Engishiki shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}){{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. Originally it was enshrined at "Setao" (Semonto Hill / Setoo), between {{ill|lt=Takachiho Peak|Q3514128|WD=Q3514128|1=Mount Takachiho}} and {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|WD=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}}, in present-day {{ill|lt=Takaharu|Q1206617|WD=Q1206617|1=Takaharu, Miyazaki}}, Nishimorokata District, Miyazaki. It was therefore also called '''Setao Gongen''', and its betto temple was said to be Setao-ji. Around the Tenkei and Tenryaku eras, priest {{ill|lt=Shoku|Q11491623|WD=Q11491623|1=Shōkū}} is said to have founded four other shrines including Himamori Shrine around the mountain foothills, and together with Kirishimamine Shrine (also called the Central Gongen Shrine of Mt. Kirishima) they were called the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=shinbutsu shugo|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, {{ill|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|Q239847|WD=Q239847|1=Vairocana}} was enshrined at Setao-ji on the approach, and the shrine came to be called '''Kirishima-san Chuo Rokusho (Rokusha) Gongen''' ('''Kirishimamine Shrine'''). As the name suggests, it became the center of the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}, and Kirishima worship flourished. In {{ill|lt=1112|Q19525|WD=Q19525|1=1112}} ({{ill|lt=Tenei|Q1193915|WD=Q1193915|1=Ten'ei}} 3), eruption of {{ill|lt=Karakuni-dake|Q3321904|WD=Q3321904|1=Mount Karakuni}}, and in {{ill|lt=1167|Q19678|WD=Q19678|1=1167}} ({{ill|lt=Nin'an (Japan)|Q3577285|WD=Q3577285|lt=Nin'an|1=Nin'an}} 2), eruption of Ōhata-yama burned shrine buildings, but each time they were rebuilt on the original site. In {{ill|lt=1234|Q5424|WD=Q5424|1=1234}} ({{ill|lt=Bunryaku|Q1194125|WD=Q1194125|1=Bunryaku}} 1), a nearby eruption of {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|WD=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}} again burned the shrine, and because a spring called Ama-no-I from this highland site dried up, it was moved near the branch shrine Kiribe Ōji Shrine, and that place was called "Shin-Setao". However, in {{ill|lt=1716|Q6892|WD=Q6892|1=1716}} ({{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 1), eruption of {{ill|lt=Shinmoedake|Q514392|WD=Q514392|1=Shinmoedake}} burned it again. The sacred body was first moved to Imabo Gongen and then to Okahara in Hosono village, but as the "Shin-Setao" site became completely deserted, in {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952|1=1729}} (Kyoho 14) shrine buildings were rebuilt on the mid-slope of {{ill|lt=Hinamori-dake|Q10942052|WD=Q10942052|1=Hinamori Dake}} and remained there until early Meiji. That location is still called Tsukiji{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. It was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} in {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}} (Meiji 5), and after combining {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hinamori Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (also counted among {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}) in {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6), it was moved to the former site of Hinamori Shrine (present location), where it remains today. In ''{{ill|lt=Sangoku Meisho Zue|Q11354726|WD=Q11354726|1=Sangoku meisho zue}}'', a record of famous places in {{ill|lt=Satsuma Domain|Q676187|WD=Q676187|1=Satsuma Domain}}, it is written as "'''Kirishima Dai-gongen-gu'''". Since ancient times it flourished as a noted shrine worshipped by the clans that ruled this area, including the {{ill|lt=Shimazu clan|Q1061406|WD=Q1061406|1=Shimazu clan}}, {{ill|lt=Ito clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280|1=Itō clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Kitahara clan|Q11401254|WD=Q11401254|1=Kitahara clan}}. {{ill|lt=Shimazu Yoshihiro|Q1156288|WD=Q1156288|1=Shimazu Yoshihiro}} is also said to have worshipped here while based at {{ill|lt=Iino Castle|Q11666711|WD=Q11666711|1=Iino Castle}}, and to have donated 100 hyo of rice each year as shrine lands. === Cultural properties === * Kobayashi City designated tangible cultural property ** Six sacred statues of Kirishimamine === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * ''Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records'', Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office, 1988 * Tatsuhiko Kuroiwa, "Kirishimamine Shrine" (in Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture Editorial Committee, ''Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture'', Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, p.251) * ''Kobayashi City History Vol.3'' (Kobayashi City History Editorial Committee, 2000) === See also === * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Shrine|Q11660899|WD=Q11660899|1=Kirishima Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}} **'''Kirishimamine Shrine''' **'''Hinamori Shrine''' **{{ill|lt=Sano Shrine|Q3472357|WD=Q3472357|1=Sano Shrine}} **Higashi Kirishima Shrine **Kirishima Higashi Shrine **{{ill|lt=Kirishima Jingū|Q704622|WD=Q704622|1=Kirishima Jingū}} * {{ill|lt=Hoko-in (Kobayashi)|Q17219459|WD=Q17219459|lt=Hoko-in|1=Hoko-in}} - former betto temple before Hinamori Gongen was merged into Kirishimamine Shrine {{神道 横}} === External links === * [https://minezinzya.wixsite.com/kirishimamine Official website of Kirishimamine Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=宮崎県}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hyūga Province]] [[Category:宮崎県の神社]] [[Category:日向国の式内社]] [[Category:山岳信仰]] [[Category:宮崎県の旧県社]] [[Category:小林市の建築物]] [[Category:小林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

[[ja:霧島岑神社]] {{Expand Japanese|霧島岑神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kirishimamine Shrine | native_name = 霧島岑神社 | image = Kirishimamine Jinja Haiden.jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Kirishimamine Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|31.966168|130.957965}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kirishimamine Shrine'''|霧島岑神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. {{ill|Q135022904|ltq=Q135022904|WD=Q135022904|1=Shikinai Ronsha}} == street address (P6375) == * {'text': '宮崎県小林市細野4937', 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:霧島岑神社]] {{wikidata link|Q11660876}} == Japanese content == {{神社 |名称 = Kirishimamine Shrine |画像 = [[Image:Kirishimamine Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|Haiden of Kirishimamine Shrine]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|Q130300|WD=Q130300|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kobayashi|Q861283|WD=Q861283|1=Kobayashi, Miyazaki}} Hosono 4937 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|31|57|57.9|N|130|57|28.3|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}

{{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (small)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} (candidate shrine)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown
tradition: mid-{{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|WD=Q8090|1=6th century}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Central six-shrine gongen of Mt. Kirishima |札所等 = Kirishima Rokusha Gongen |例祭 = November 18 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Miyazaki|label=Kirishimamine Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=11|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Kirishimamine Shrine''' (きりしまみねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hosono, {{ill|lt=Kobayashi|Q861283|WD=Q861283|1=Kobayashi, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|Q130300|WD=Q130300|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. It is a candidate shrine for an {{ill|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}Kobayashi City History, Vol.3, pp.1095-1097 * {{ill|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} and {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyuga mythology)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} Ninigi, Hikohohodemi, and Ugayafukiaezu are called the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|WD=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}, ancestors of the {{ill|lt=Imperial Household|Q909452|WD=Q909452|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, and the other three are their consorts. === Origin === [[Image:Kirishimamine Jinja Sando.jpg|thumb|270px|Approach]]{{出典の明記| date = 2022年2月| section = 1}} The founding date is unknown, though tradition places it around the mid-6th century. It is regarded as a shrine deeply connected with the descent of the heavenly grandson ({{ill|lt=Tenson kōrin|Q1084733|WD=Q1084733|1=Tenson kōrin}}) and the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|WD=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. According to shrine tradition, the "Kirishimamine deity" in the entry for August 1, {{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093|1=837}} ({{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 4) of the ''{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}'' - where Miyazaki's Tonō, Tsuma, Eda, and Kirishimamine deities were granted status as official shrines - refers to this shrine{{Cite book|和書 |title=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records |date=1988-09-30|year=1988|publisher=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. In ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'', it is stated that in {{ill|lt=858|Q30371|WD=Q30371|1=858}} ({{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} 2), it was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower. There is also a theory identifying this shrine with "Kirishima Shrine" in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' for Morokata District, Hyuga Province (thus a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=candidate for Engishiki shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}){{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. Originally it was enshrined at "Setao" (Semonto Hill / Setoo), between {{ill|lt=Takachiho Peak|Q3514128|WD=Q3514128|1=Mount Takachiho}} and {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|WD=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}}, in present-day {{ill|lt=Takaharu|Q1206617|WD=Q1206617|1=Takaharu, Miyazaki}}, Nishimorokata District, Miyazaki. It was therefore also called '''Setao Gongen''', and its betto temple was said to be Setao-ji. Around the Tenkei and Tenryaku eras, priest {{ill|lt=Shoku|Q11491623|WD=Q11491623|1=Shōkū}} is said to have founded four other shrines including Himamori Shrine around the mountain foothills, and together with Kirishimamine Shrine (also called the Central Gongen Shrine of Mt. Kirishima) they were called the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=shinbutsu shugo|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, {{ill|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|Q239847|WD=Q239847|1=Vairocana}} was enshrined at Setao-ji on the approach, and the shrine came to be called '''Kirishima-san Chuo Rokusho (Rokusha) Gongen''' ('''Kirishimamine Shrine'''). As the name suggests, it became the center of the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}, and Kirishima worship flourished. In {{ill|lt=1112|Q19525|WD=Q19525|1=1112}} ({{ill|lt=Tenei|Q1193915|WD=Q1193915|1=Ten'ei}} 3), eruption of {{ill|lt=Karakuni-dake|Q3321904|WD=Q3321904|1=Mount Karakuni}}, and in {{ill|lt=1167|Q19678|WD=Q19678|1=1167}} ({{ill|lt=Nin'an (Japan)|Q3577285|WD=Q3577285|lt=Nin'an|1=Nin'an}} 2), eruption of Ōhata-yama burned shrine buildings, but each time they were rebuilt on the original site. In {{ill|lt=1234|Q5424|WD=Q5424|1=1234}} ({{ill|lt=Bunryaku|Q1194125|WD=Q1194125|1=Bunryaku}} 1), a nearby eruption of {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|WD=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}} again burned the shrine, and because a spring called Ama-no-I from this highland site dried up, it was moved near the branch shrine Kiribe Ōji Shrine, and that place was called "Shin-Setao". However, in {{ill|lt=1716|Q6892|WD=Q6892|1=1716}} ({{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 1), eruption of {{ill|lt=Shinmoedake|Q514392|WD=Q514392|1=Shinmoedake}} burned it again. The sacred body was first moved to Imabo Gongen and then to Okahara in Hosono village, but as the "Shin-Setao" site became completely deserted, in {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952|1=1729}} (Kyoho 14) shrine buildings were rebuilt on the mid-slope of {{ill|lt=Hinamori-dake|Q10942052|WD=Q10942052|1=Hinamori Dake}} and remained there until early Meiji. That location is still called Tsukiji{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. It was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} in {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}} (Meiji 5), and after combining {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hinamori Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (also counted among {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}) in {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6), it was moved to the former site of Hinamori Shrine (present location), where it remains today. In ''{{ill|lt=Sangoku Meisho Zue|Q11354726|WD=Q11354726|1=Sangoku meisho zue}}'', a record of famous places in {{ill|lt=Satsuma Domain|Q676187|WD=Q676187|1=Satsuma Domain}}, it is written as "'''Kirishima Dai-gongen-gu'''". Since ancient times it flourished as a noted shrine worshipped by the clans that ruled this area, including the {{ill|lt=Shimazu clan|Q1061406|WD=Q1061406|1=Shimazu clan}}, {{ill|lt=Ito clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280|1=Itō clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Kitahara clan|Q11401254|WD=Q11401254|1=Kitahara clan}}. {{ill|lt=Shimazu Yoshihiro|Q1156288|WD=Q1156288|1=Shimazu Yoshihiro}} is also said to have worshipped here while based at {{ill|lt=Iino Castle|Q11666711|WD=Q11666711|1=Iino Castle}}, and to have donated 100 hyo of rice each year as shrine lands. === Cultural properties === * Kobayashi City designated tangible cultural property ** Six sacred statues of Kirishimamine === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * ''Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records'', Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office, 1988 * Tatsuhiko Kuroiwa, "Kirishimamine Shrine" (in Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture Editorial Committee, ''Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture'', Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, p.251) * ''Kobayashi City History Vol.3'' (Kobayashi City History Editorial Committee, 2000) === See also === * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Shrine|Q11660899|WD=Q11660899|1=Kirishima Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}} **'''Kirishimamine Shrine''' **'''Hinamori Shrine''' **{{ill|lt=Sano Shrine|Q3472357|WD=Q3472357|1=Sano Shrine}} **Higashi Kirishima Shrine **Kirishima Higashi Shrine **{{ill|lt=Kirishima Jingū|Q704622|WD=Q704622|1=Kirishima Jingū}} * {{ill|lt=Hoko-in (Kobayashi)|Q17219459|WD=Q17219459|lt=Hoko-in|1=Hoko-in}} - former betto temple before Hinamori Gongen was merged into Kirishimamine Shrine {{神道 横}} === External links === * [https://minezinzya.wixsite.com/kirishimamine Official website of Kirishimamine Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=宮崎県}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hyūga Province]] [[Category:宮崎県の神社]] [[Category:日向国の式内社]] [[Category:山岳信仰]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:小林市の建築物]] [[Category:小林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

tradition: mid-{{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|WD=Q8090|1=6th century}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Central six-shrine gongen of Mt. Kirishima |札所等 = Kirishima Rokusha Gongen |例祭 = November 18 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Miyazaki|label=Kirishimamine Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=11|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Kirishimamine Shrine''' (きりしまみねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hosono, {{ill|lt=Kobayashi|Q861283|WD=Q861283|1=Kobayashi, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|Q130300|WD=Q130300|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. It is a candidate shrine for an {{ill|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}Kobayashi City History, Vol.3, pp.1095-1097 * {{ill|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} and {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyuga mythology)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} Ninigi, Hikohohodemi, and Ugayafukiaezu are called the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|WD=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}, ancestors of the {{ill|lt=Imperial Household|Q909452|WD=Q909452|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, and the other three are their consorts. === Origin === [[Image:Kirishimamine Jinja Sando.jpg|thumb|270px|Approach]]{{出典の明記| date = 2022年2月| section = 1}} The founding date is unknown, though tradition places it around the mid-6th century. It is regarded as a shrine deeply connected with the descent of the heavenly grandson ({{ill|lt=Tenson kōrin|Q1084733|WD=Q1084733|1=Tenson kōrin}}) and the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|WD=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. According to shrine tradition, the "Kirishimamine deity" in the entry for August 1, {{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093|1=837}} ({{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 4) of the ''{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}'' - where Miyazaki's Tonō, Tsuma, Eda, and Kirishimamine deities were granted status as official shrines - refers to this shrine{{Cite book|和書 |title=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records |date=1988-09-30|year=1988|publisher=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. In ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'', it is stated that in {{ill|lt=858|Q30371|WD=Q30371|1=858}} ({{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} 2), it was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower. There is also a theory identifying this shrine with "Kirishima Shrine" in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' for Morokata District, Hyuga Province (thus a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=candidate for Engishiki shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}){{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. Originally it was enshrined at "Setao" (Semonto Hill / Setoo), between {{ill|lt=Takachiho Peak|Q3514128|WD=Q3514128|1=Mount Takachiho}} and {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|WD=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}}, in present-day {{ill|lt=Takaharu|Q1206617|WD=Q1206617|1=Takaharu, Miyazaki}}, Nishimorokata District, Miyazaki. It was therefore also called '''Setao Gongen''', and its betto temple was said to be Setao-ji. Around the Tenkei and Tenryaku eras, priest {{ill|lt=Shoku|Q11491623|WD=Q11491623|1=Shōkū}} is said to have founded four other shrines including Himamori Shrine around the mountain foothills, and together with Kirishimamine Shrine (also called the Central Gongen Shrine of Mt. Kirishima) they were called the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=shinbutsu shugo|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, {{ill|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|Q239847|WD=Q239847|1=Vairocana}} was enshrined at Setao-ji on the approach, and the shrine came to be called '''Kirishima-san Chuo Rokusho (Rokusha) Gongen''' ('''Kirishimamine Shrine'''). As the name suggests, it became the center of the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}, and Kirishima worship flourished. In {{ill|lt=1112|Q19525|WD=Q19525|1=1112}} ({{ill|lt=Tenei|Q1193915|WD=Q1193915|1=Ten'ei}} 3), eruption of {{ill|lt=Karakuni-dake|Q3321904|WD=Q3321904|1=Mount Karakuni}}, and in {{ill|lt=1167|Q19678|WD=Q19678|1=1167}} ({{ill|lt=Nin'an (Japan)|Q3577285|WD=Q3577285|lt=Nin'an|1=Nin'an}} 2), eruption of Ōhata-yama burned shrine buildings, but each time they were rebuilt on the original site. In {{ill|lt=1234|Q5424|WD=Q5424|1=1234}} ({{ill|lt=Bunryaku|Q1194125|WD=Q1194125|1=Bunryaku}} 1), a nearby eruption of {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|WD=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}} again burned the shrine, and because a spring called Ama-no-I from this highland site dried up, it was moved near the branch shrine Kiribe Ōji Shrine, and that place was called "Shin-Setao". However, in {{ill|lt=1716|Q6892|WD=Q6892|1=1716}} ({{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 1), eruption of {{ill|lt=Shinmoedake|Q514392|WD=Q514392|1=Shinmoedake}} burned it again. The sacred body was first moved to Imabo Gongen and then to Okahara in Hosono village, but as the "Shin-Setao" site became completely deserted, in {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952|1=1729}} (Kyoho 14) shrine buildings were rebuilt on the mid-slope of {{ill|lt=Hinamori-dake|Q10942052|WD=Q10942052|1=Hinamori Dake}} and remained there until early Meiji. That location is still called Tsukiji{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. It was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} in {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}} (Meiji 5), and after combining {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hinamori Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (also counted among {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}) in {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6), it was moved to the former site of Hinamori Shrine (present location), where it remains today. In ''{{ill|lt=Sangoku Meisho Zue|Q11354726|WD=Q11354726|1=Sangoku meisho zue}}'', a record of famous places in {{ill|lt=Satsuma Domain|Q676187|WD=Q676187|1=Satsuma Domain}}, it is written as "'''Kirishima Dai-gongen-gu'''". Since ancient times it flourished as a noted shrine worshipped by the clans that ruled this area, including the {{ill|lt=Shimazu clan|Q1061406|WD=Q1061406|1=Shimazu clan}}, {{ill|lt=Ito clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280|1=Itō clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Kitahara clan|Q11401254|WD=Q11401254|1=Kitahara clan}}. {{ill|lt=Shimazu Yoshihiro|Q1156288|WD=Q1156288|1=Shimazu Yoshihiro}} is also said to have worshipped here while based at {{ill|lt=Iino Castle|Q11666711|WD=Q11666711|1=Iino Castle}}, and to have donated 100 hyo of rice each year as shrine lands. === Cultural properties === * Kobayashi City designated tangible cultural property ** Six sacred statues of Kirishimamine === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * ''Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records'', Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office, 1988 * Tatsuhiko Kuroiwa, "Kirishimamine Shrine" (in Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture Editorial Committee, ''Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture'', Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, p.251) * ''Kobayashi City History Vol.3'' (Kobayashi City History Editorial Committee, 2000) === See also === * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Shrine|Q11660899|WD=Q11660899|1=Kirishima Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}} **'''Kirishimamine Shrine''' **'''Hinamori Shrine''' **{{ill|lt=Sano Shrine|Q3472357|WD=Q3472357|1=Sano Shrine}} **Higashi Kirishima Shrine **Kirishima Higashi Shrine **{{ill|lt=Kirishima Jingū|Q704622|WD=Q704622|1=Kirishima Jingū}} * {{ill|lt=Hoko-in (Kobayashi)|Q17219459|WD=Q17219459|lt=Hoko-in|1=Hoko-in}} - former betto temple before Hinamori Gongen was merged into Kirishimamine Shrine {{神道 横}} === External links === * [https://minezinzya.wixsite.com/kirishimamine Official website of Kirishimamine Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=宮崎県}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hyūga Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:日向国の式内社]] [[Category:山岳信仰]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:小林市の建築物]] [[Category:小林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

== {{神社 |名称 = Kirishimamine Shrine |画像 = [[Image:Kirishimamine Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|Haiden of Kirishimamine Shrine]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|Q130300|WD=Q130300|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kobayashi|Q861283|WD=Q861283|1=Kobayashi, Miyazaki}} Hosono 4937 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|31|57|57.9|N|130|57|28.3|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}

{{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}}
{{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}}
{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} |社格 = {{ill|lt=Shikinaisha (small)|Q134917287|WD=Q134917287|1=Shikinai Shosha}} (candidate shrine)
former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown
tradition: mid-{{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|WD=Q8090|1=6th century}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Central six-shrine gongen of Mt. Kirishima |札所等 = Kirishima Rokusha Gongen |例祭 = November 18 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Miyazaki|label=Kirishimamine Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=11|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Kirishimamine Shrine''' (きりしまみねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hosono, {{ill|lt=Kobayashi|Q861283|WD=Q861283|1=Kobayashi, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|Q130300|WD=Q130300|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. It is a candidate shrine for an {{ill|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}Kobayashi City History, Vol.3, pp.1095-1097 * {{ill|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} and {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyuga mythology)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} Ninigi, Hikohohodemi, and Ugayafukiaezu are called the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|WD=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}, ancestors of the {{ill|lt=Imperial Household|Q909452|WD=Q909452|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, and the other three are their consorts. === Origin === [[Image:Kirishimamine Jinja Sando.jpg|thumb|270px|Approach]]{{出典の明記| date = 2022年2月| section = 1}} The founding date is unknown, though tradition places it around the mid-6th century. It is regarded as a shrine deeply connected with the descent of the heavenly grandson ({{ill|lt=Tenson kōrin|Q1084733|WD=Q1084733|1=Tenson kōrin}}) and the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|WD=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. According to shrine tradition, the "Kirishimamine deity" in the entry for August 1, {{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093|1=837}} ({{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 4) of the ''{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}'' - where Miyazaki's Tonō, Tsuma, Eda, and Kirishimamine deities were granted status as official shrines - refers to this shrine{{Cite book|和書 |title=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records |date=1988-09-30|year=1988|publisher=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. In ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'', it is stated that in {{ill|lt=858|Q30371|WD=Q30371|1=858}} ({{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} 2), it was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower. There is also a theory identifying this shrine with "Kirishima Shrine" in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' for Morokata District, Hyuga Province (thus a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=candidate for Engishiki shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}){{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. Originally it was enshrined at "Setao" (Semonto Hill / Setoo), between {{ill|lt=Takachiho Peak|Q3514128|WD=Q3514128|1=Mount Takachiho}} and {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|WD=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}}, in present-day {{ill|lt=Takaharu|Q1206617|WD=Q1206617|1=Takaharu, Miyazaki}}, Nishimorokata District, Miyazaki. It was therefore also called '''Setao Gongen''', and its betto temple was said to be Setao-ji. Around the Tenkei and Tenryaku eras, priest {{ill|lt=Shoku|Q11491623|WD=Q11491623|1=Shōkū}} is said to have founded four other shrines including Himamori Shrine around the mountain foothills, and together with Kirishimamine Shrine (also called the Central Gongen Shrine of Mt. Kirishima) they were called the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=shinbutsu shugo|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, {{ill|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|Q239847|WD=Q239847|1=Vairocana}} was enshrined at Setao-ji on the approach, and the shrine came to be called '''Kirishima-san Chuo Rokusho (Rokusha) Gongen''' ('''Kirishimamine Shrine'''). As the name suggests, it became the center of the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}, and Kirishima worship flourished. In {{ill|lt=1112|Q19525|WD=Q19525|1=1112}} ({{ill|lt=Tenei|Q1193915|WD=Q1193915|1=Ten'ei}} 3), eruption of {{ill|lt=Karakuni-dake|Q3321904|WD=Q3321904|1=Mount Karakuni}}, and in {{ill|lt=1167|Q19678|WD=Q19678|1=1167}} ({{ill|lt=Nin'an (Japan)|Q3577285|WD=Q3577285|lt=Nin'an|1=Nin'an}} 2), eruption of Ōhata-yama burned shrine buildings, but each time they were rebuilt on the original site. In {{ill|lt=1234|Q5424|WD=Q5424|1=1234}} ({{ill|lt=Bunryaku|Q1194125|WD=Q1194125|1=Bunryaku}} 1), a nearby eruption of {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|WD=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}} again burned the shrine, and because a spring called Ama-no-I from this highland site dried up, it was moved near the branch shrine Kiribe Ōji Shrine, and that place was called "Shin-Setao". However, in {{ill|lt=1716|Q6892|WD=Q6892|1=1716}} ({{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 1), eruption of {{ill|lt=Shinmoedake|Q514392|WD=Q514392|1=Shinmoedake}} burned it again. The sacred body was first moved to Imabo Gongen and then to Okahara in Hosono village, but as the "Shin-Setao" site became completely deserted, in {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952|1=1729}} (Kyoho 14) shrine buildings were rebuilt on the mid-slope of {{ill|lt=Hinamori-dake|Q10942052|WD=Q10942052|1=Hinamori Dake}} and remained there until early Meiji. That location is still called Tsukiji{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. It was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} in {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}} (Meiji 5), and after combining {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hinamori Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (also counted among {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}) in {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6), it was moved to the former site of Hinamori Shrine (present location), where it remains today. In ''{{ill|lt=Sangoku Meisho Zue|Q11354726|WD=Q11354726|1=Sangoku meisho zue}}'', a record of famous places in {{ill|lt=Satsuma Domain|Q676187|WD=Q676187|1=Satsuma Domain}}, it is written as "'''Kirishima Dai-gongen-gu'''". Since ancient times it flourished as a noted shrine worshipped by the clans that ruled this area, including the {{ill|lt=Shimazu clan|Q1061406|WD=Q1061406|1=Shimazu clan}}, {{ill|lt=Ito clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280|1=Itō clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Kitahara clan|Q11401254|WD=Q11401254|1=Kitahara clan}}. {{ill|lt=Shimazu Yoshihiro|Q1156288|WD=Q1156288|1=Shimazu Yoshihiro}} is also said to have worshipped here while based at {{ill|lt=Iino Castle|Q11666711|WD=Q11666711|1=Iino Castle}}, and to have donated 100 hyo of rice each year as shrine lands. === Cultural properties === * Kobayashi City designated tangible cultural property ** Six sacred statues of Kirishimamine === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * ''Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records'', Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office, 1988 * Tatsuhiko Kuroiwa, "Kirishimamine Shrine" (in Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture Editorial Committee, ''Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture'', Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, p.251) * ''Kobayashi City History Vol.3'' (Kobayashi City History Editorial Committee, 2000) === See also === * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Shrine|Q11660899|WD=Q11660899|1=Kirishima Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}} **'''Kirishimamine Shrine''' **'''Hinamori Shrine''' **{{ill|lt=Sano Shrine|Q3472357|WD=Q3472357|1=Sano Shrine}} **Higashi Kirishima Shrine **Kirishima Higashi Shrine **{{ill|lt=Kirishima Jingū|Q704622|WD=Q704622|1=Kirishima Jingū}} * {{ill|lt=Hoko-in (Kobayashi)|Q17219459|WD=Q17219459|lt=Hoko-in|1=Hoko-in}} - former betto temple before Hinamori Gongen was merged into Kirishimamine Shrine {{神道 横}} === External links === * [https://minezinzya.wixsite.com/kirishimamine Official website of Kirishimamine Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=宮崎県}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hyūga Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hyūga Province]] [[Category:山岳信仰]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:小林市の建築物]] [[Category:小林市の歴史]] [[Category:pages]]

== {{神社 |名称 = Kirishimamine Shrine |画像 = [[Image:Kirishimamine Jinja Haiden.jpg|270px|Haiden of Kirishimamine Shrine]]
Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|Q130300|WD=Q130300|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kobayashi|Q861283|WD=Q861283|1=Kobayashi, Miyazaki}} Hosono 4937 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|31|57|57.9|N|130|57|28.3|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}

former {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} |創建 = Unknown
tradition: mid-{{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|WD=Q8090|1=6th century}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Central six-shrine gongen of Mt. Kirishima |札所等 = Kirishima Rokusha Gongen |例祭 = November 18 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Miyazaki|label=Kirishimamine Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=11|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Kirishimamine Shrine''' (きりしまみねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|WD=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hosono, {{ill|lt=Kobayashi|Q861283|WD=Q861283|1=Kobayashi, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|Q130300|WD=Q130300|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. It is a candidate shrine for an {{ill|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}Kobayashi City History, Vol.3, pp.1095-1097 * {{ill|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|WD=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|Q1373935|WD=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} and {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyuga mythology)|Q3082751|WD=Q3082751|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} Ninigi, Hikohohodemi, and Ugayafukiaezu are called the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|WD=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}, ancestors of the {{ill|lt=Imperial Household|Q909452|WD=Q909452|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, and the other three are their consorts. === Origin === [[Image:Kirishimamine Jinja Sando.jpg|thumb|270px|Approach]]{{出典の明記| date = 2022年2月| section = 1}} The founding date is unknown, though tradition places it around the mid-6th century. It is regarded as a shrine deeply connected with the descent of the heavenly grandson ({{ill|lt=Tenson kōrin|Q1084733|WD=Q1084733|1=Tenson kōrin}}) and the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|WD=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. According to shrine tradition, the "Kirishimamine deity" in the entry for August 1, {{ill|lt=837|Q25093|WD=Q25093|1=837}} ({{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|WD=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 4) of the ''{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|WD=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}'' - where Miyazaki's Tonō, Tsuma, Eda, and Kirishimamine deities were granted status as official shrines - refers to this shrine{{Cite book|和書 |title=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records |date=1988-09-30|year=1988|publisher=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. In ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|WD=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'', it is stated that in {{ill|lt=858|Q30371|WD=Q30371|1=858}} ({{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|WD=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} 2), it was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower. There is also a theory identifying this shrine with "Kirishima Shrine" in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' for Morokata District, Hyuga Province (thus a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=candidate for Engishiki shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}){{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. Originally it was enshrined at "Setao" (Semonto Hill / Setoo), between {{ill|lt=Takachiho Peak|Q3514128|WD=Q3514128|1=Mount Takachiho}} and {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|WD=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}}, in present-day {{ill|lt=Takaharu|Q1206617|WD=Q1206617|1=Takaharu, Miyazaki}}, Nishimorokata District, Miyazaki. It was therefore also called '''Setao Gongen''', and its betto temple was said to be Setao-ji. Around the Tenkei and Tenryaku eras, priest {{ill|lt=Shoku|Q11491623|WD=Q11491623|1=Shōkū}} is said to have founded four other shrines including Himamori Shrine around the mountain foothills, and together with Kirishimamine Shrine (also called the Central Gongen Shrine of Mt. Kirishima) they were called the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=shinbutsu shugo|Q2279088|WD=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, {{ill|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|Q239847|WD=Q239847|1=Vairocana}} was enshrined at Setao-ji on the approach, and the shrine came to be called '''Kirishima-san Chuo Rokusho (Rokusha) Gongen''' ('''Kirishimamine Shrine'''). As the name suggests, it became the center of the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}, and Kirishima worship flourished. In {{ill|lt=1112|Q19525|WD=Q19525|1=1112}} ({{ill|lt=Tenei|Q1193915|WD=Q1193915|1=Ten'ei}} 3), eruption of {{ill|lt=Karakuni-dake|Q3321904|WD=Q3321904|1=Mount Karakuni}}, and in {{ill|lt=1167|Q19678|WD=Q19678|1=1167}} ({{ill|lt=Nin'an (Japan)|Q3577285|WD=Q3577285|lt=Nin'an|1=Nin'an}} 2), eruption of Ōhata-yama burned shrine buildings, but each time they were rebuilt on the original site. In {{ill|lt=1234|Q5424|WD=Q5424|1=1234}} ({{ill|lt=Bunryaku|Q1194125|WD=Q1194125|1=Bunryaku}} 1), a nearby eruption of {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|WD=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}} again burned the shrine, and because a spring called Ama-no-I from this highland site dried up, it was moved near the branch shrine Kiribe Ōji Shrine, and that place was called "Shin-Setao". However, in {{ill|lt=1716|Q6892|WD=Q6892|1=1716}} ({{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|WD=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 1), eruption of {{ill|lt=Shinmoedake|Q514392|WD=Q514392|1=Shinmoedake}} burned it again. The sacred body was first moved to Imabo Gongen and then to Okahara in Hosono village, but as the "Shin-Setao" site became completely deserted, in {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|WD=Q6952|1=1729}} (Kyoho 14) shrine buildings were rebuilt on the mid-slope of {{ill|lt=Hinamori-dake|Q10942052|WD=Q10942052|1=Hinamori Dake}} and remained there until early Meiji. That location is still called Tsukiji{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. It was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|WD=Q90139226}} in {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753|1=1872}} (Meiji 5), and after combining {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hinamori Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (also counted among {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}) in {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|WD=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6), it was moved to the former site of Hinamori Shrine (present location), where it remains today. In ''{{ill|lt=Sangoku Meisho Zue|Q11354726|WD=Q11354726|1=Sangoku meisho zue}}'', a record of famous places in {{ill|lt=Satsuma Domain|Q676187|WD=Q676187|1=Satsuma Domain}}, it is written as "'''Kirishima Dai-gongen-gu'''". Since ancient times it flourished as a noted shrine worshipped by the clans that ruled this area, including the {{ill|lt=Shimazu clan|Q1061406|WD=Q1061406|1=Shimazu clan}}, {{ill|lt=Ito clan|Q2975280|WD=Q2975280|1=Itō clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Kitahara clan|Q11401254|WD=Q11401254|1=Kitahara clan}}. {{ill|lt=Shimazu Yoshihiro|Q1156288|WD=Q1156288|1=Shimazu Yoshihiro}} is also said to have worshipped here while based at {{ill|lt=Iino Castle|Q11666711|WD=Q11666711|1=Iino Castle}}, and to have donated 100 hyo of rice each year as shrine lands. === Cultural properties === * Kobayashi City designated tangible cultural property ** Six sacred statues of Kirishimamine === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * ''Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records'', Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office, 1988 * Tatsuhiko Kuroiwa, "Kirishimamine Shrine" (in Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture Editorial Committee, ''Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture'', Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, p.251) * ''Kobayashi City History Vol.3'' (Kobayashi City History Editorial Committee, 2000) === See also === * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Shrine|Q11660899|WD=Q11660899|1=Kirishima Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|WD=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}} **'''Kirishimamine Shrine''' **'''Hinamori Shrine''' **{{ill|lt=Sano Shrine|Q3472357|WD=Q3472357|1=Sano Shrine}} **Higashi Kirishima Shrine **Kirishima Higashi Shrine **{{ill|lt=Kirishima Jingū|Q704622|WD=Q704622|1=Kirishima Jingū}} * {{ill|lt=Hoko-in (Kobayashi)|Q17219459|WD=Q17219459|lt=Hoko-in|1=Hoko-in}} - former betto temple before Hinamori Gongen was merged into Kirishimamine Shrine {{神道 横}} === External links === * [https://minezinzya.wixsite.com/kirishimamine Official website of Kirishimamine Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=宮崎県}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hyūga Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hyūga Province]] [[Category:山岳信仰]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:小林市の建築物]] [[Category:小林市の歴史]]

[[ja:霧島岑神社]] {{Expand Japanese|霧島岑神社}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Kirishimamine Shrine | native_name = 霧島岑神社 | image = Kirishimamine Jinja Haiden.jpg | mapframe = yes | mapframe-zoom = 15 | mapframe-wikidata = yes | mapframe-point = none | map_type = Japan | map_caption = Kirishimamine Shrine | coordinates = {{coord|31.966168|130.957965}} | map_relief = 1 | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] }} {{nihongo|'''Kirishimamine Shrine'''|霧島岑神社}} is a [[shinto shrine]] in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]]. Haiden |所在地 = {{ill|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|Q130300|qid=Q130300|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}{{ill|lt=Kobayashi|Q861283|qid=Q861283|1=Kobayashi, Miyazaki}} Hosono 4937 |位置 = {{ウィキ座標2段度分秒|31|57|57.9|N|130|57|28.3|E|display=inline,title}} |祭神 = {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}

tradition: mid-{{ill|lt=6th century|Q8090|qid=Q8090|1=6th century}} |本殿 = {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|qid=Q210684|1=Nagare-zukuri}} |別名 = Central six-shrine gongen of Mt. Kirishima |札所等 = Kirishima Rokusha Gongen |例祭 = November 18 |神事 = |地図 = {{Location map|Japan Miyazaki|label=Kirishimamine Shrine|position=right|width=200|float=center|caption=|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=20}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|marker=religious-shinto|zoom=11|frame-align=center|frame-width=200}} }} '''Kirishimamine Shrine''' (きりしまみねじんじゃ) is a {{ill|lt=Shinto shrine|Q845945|qid=Q845945|1=Shinto shrine}} in Hosono, {{ill|lt=Kobayashi|Q861283|qid=Q861283|1=Kobayashi, Miyazaki}}, {{ill|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|Q130300|qid=Q130300|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. It is a candidate shrine for an {{ill|lt=Engishiki-listed shrine|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}, and its former rank was {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}}. === Enshrined deities === * {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Ninigi no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Konohanasakuya-hime no Mikoto|1=UNKNOWN}}Kobayashi City History, Vol.3, pp.1095-1097 * {{ill|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|UNKNOWN|lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|qid=Q2329261|lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|1=Toyotama-hime}} * {{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu|Q1373935|qid=Q1373935|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto|1=Ugayafukiaezu}} and {{ill|lt=Tamayoribime (Hyuga mythology)|Q3082751|qid=Q3082751|lt=Tamayori-hime no Mikoto|1=Tamayori-hime (mother of Jimmu)}} Ninigi, Hikohohodemi, and Ugayafukiaezu are called the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|qid=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}, ancestors of the {{ill|lt=Imperial Household|Q909452|qid=Q909452|1=Imperial House of Japan}}, and the other three are their consorts. === Origin === [[Image:Kirishimamine Jinja Sando.jpg|thumb|270px|Approach]]{{出典の明記| date = 2022年2月| section = 1}} The founding date is unknown, though tradition places it around the mid-6th century. It is regarded as a shrine deeply connected with the descent of the heavenly grandson ({{ill|lt=Tenson kōrin|Q1084733|qid=Q1084733|1=Tenson kōrin}}) and the {{ill|lt=Three Generations of Hyuga|Q56347075|qid=Q56347075|1=Three generations of Hyuga}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. According to shrine tradition, the "Kirishimamine deity" in the entry for August 1, {{ill|lt=837|Q25093|qid=Q25093|1=837}} ({{ill|lt=Jowa (Japan)|Q1075605|qid=Q1075605|lt=Jōwa|1=Jōwa (Heian period)}} 4) of the ''{{ill|lt=Shoku Nihon Kōki|Q1041301|qid=Q1041301|1=Shoku Nihon Kōki}}'' - where Miyazaki's Tonō, Tsuma, Eda, and Kirishimamine deities were granted status as official shrines - refers to this shrine{{Cite book|和書 |title=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records |date=1988-09-30|year=1988|publisher=Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office}}{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. In ''{{ill|lt=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|Q1052981|qid=Q1052981|1=Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku}}'', it is stated that in {{ill|lt=858|Q30371|qid=Q30371|1=858}} ({{ill|lt=Ten'an (Japan)|Q1075611|qid=Q1075611|lt=Ten'an|1=Ten'an}} 2), it was promoted to Junior Fourth Rank Lower. There is also a theory identifying this shrine with "Kirishima Shrine" in the ''{{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyocho|Q11064932|qid=Q11064932|1=Engishiki Jinmyōchō}}'' for Morokata District, Hyuga Province (thus a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=candidate for Engishiki shrine|1=UNKNOWN}}){{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. Originally it was enshrined at "Setao" (Semonto Hill / Setoo), between {{ill|lt=Takachiho Peak|Q3514128|qid=Q3514128|1=Mount Takachiho}} and {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|qid=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}}, in present-day {{ill|lt=Takaharu|Q1206617|qid=Q1206617|1=Takaharu, Miyazaki}}, Nishimorokata District, Miyazaki. It was therefore also called '''Setao Gongen''', and its betto temple was said to be Setao-ji. Around the Tenkei and Tenryaku eras, priest {{ill|lt=Shoku|Q11491623|qid=Q11491623|1=Shōkū}} is said to have founded four other shrines including Himamori Shrine around the mountain foothills, and together with Kirishimamine Shrine (also called the Central Gongen Shrine of Mt. Kirishima) they were called the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|qid=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}. During the period of {{ill|lt=shinbutsu shugo|Q2279088|qid=Q2279088|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}}, {{ill|lt=Dainichi Nyorai|Q239847|qid=Q239847|1=Vairocana}} was enshrined at Setao-ji on the approach, and the shrine came to be called '''Kirishima-san Chuo Rokusho (Rokusha) Gongen''' ('''Kirishimamine Shrine'''). As the name suggests, it became the center of the {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|qid=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}, and Kirishima worship flourished. In {{ill|lt=1112|Q19525|qid=Q19525|1=1112}} ({{ill|lt=Tenei|Q1193915|qid=Q1193915|1=Ten'ei}} 3), eruption of {{ill|lt=Karakuni-dake|Q3321904|qid=Q3321904|1=Mount Karakuni}}, and in {{ill|lt=1167|Q19678|qid=Q19678|1=1167}} ({{ill|lt=Nin'an (Japan)|Q3577285|qid=Q3577285|lt=Nin'an|1=Nin'an}} 2), eruption of Ōhata-yama burned shrine buildings, but each time they were rebuilt on the original site. In {{ill|lt=1234|Q5424|qid=Q5424|1=1234}} ({{ill|lt=Bunryaku|Q1194125|qid=Q1194125|1=Bunryaku}} 1), a nearby eruption of {{ill|lt=Onachi (Miike)|Q11489346|qid=Q11489346|lt=Hiwa-ga-mine (Miike)|1=Ohachi}} again burned the shrine, and because a spring called Ama-no-I from this highland site dried up, it was moved near the branch shrine Kiribe Ōji Shrine, and that place was called "Shin-Setao". However, in {{ill|lt=1716|Q6892|qid=Q6892|1=1716}} ({{ill|lt=Kyoho|Q1145048|qid=Q1145048|1=Kyōhō}} 1), eruption of {{ill|lt=Shinmoedake|Q514392|qid=Q514392|1=Shinmoedake}} burned it again. The sacred body was first moved to Imabo Gongen and then to Okahara in Hosono village, but as the "Shin-Setao" site became completely deserted, in {{ill|lt=1729|Q6952|qid=Q6952|1=1729}} (Kyoho 14) shrine buildings were rebuilt on the mid-slope of {{ill|lt=Hinamori-dake|Q10942052|qid=Q10942052|1=Hinamori Dake}} and remained there until early Meiji. That location is still called Tsukiji{{要出典|date=2022年2月}}. It was ranked as {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=kensha|1=Ken-sha|qid=Q90139226}} in {{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|qid=Q7753|1=1872}} (Meiji 5), and after combining {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hinamori Shrine|1=UNKNOWN}} (also counted among {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|qid=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}}) in {{ill|lt=1873|Q7757|qid=Q7757|1=1873}} (Meiji 6), it was moved to the former site of Hinamori Shrine (present location), where it remains today. In ''{{ill|lt=Sangoku Meisho Zue|Q11354726|qid=Q11354726|1=Sangoku meisho zue}}'', a record of famous places in {{ill|lt=Satsuma Domain|Q676187|qid=Q676187|1=Satsuma Domain}}, it is written as "'''Kirishima Dai-gongen-gu'''". Since ancient times it flourished as a noted shrine worshipped by the clans that ruled this area, including the {{ill|lt=Shimazu clan|Q1061406|qid=Q1061406|1=Shimazu clan}}, {{ill|lt=Ito clan|Q2975280|qid=Q2975280|1=Itō clan}}, and {{ill|lt=Kitahara clan|Q11401254|qid=Q11401254|1=Kitahara clan}}. {{ill|lt=Shimazu Yoshihiro|Q1156288|qid=Q1156288|1=Shimazu Yoshihiro}} is also said to have worshipped here while based at {{ill|lt=Iino Castle|Q11666711|qid=Q11666711|1=Iino Castle}}, and to have donated 100 hyo of rice each year as shrine lands. === Cultural properties === * Kobayashi City designated tangible cultural property ** Six sacred statues of Kirishimamine === Notes === {{reflist}} === References === * ''Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Records'', Miyazaki Prefecture Shrine Office, 1988 * Tatsuhiko Kuroiwa, "Kirishimamine Shrine" (in Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture Editorial Committee, ''Encyclopedia of Miyazaki Prefecture'', Miyazaki Nichinichi Shimbun, 1983, p.251) * ''Kobayashi City History Vol.3'' (Kobayashi City History Editorial Committee, 2000) === See also === * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Shrine|Q11660899|qid=Q11660899|1=Kirishima Shrine}} * {{ill|lt=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen|Q11660872|qid=Q11660872|1=Kirishima Rokusha Gongen}} **'''Kirishimamine Shrine''' **'''Hinamori Shrine''' **{{ill|lt=Sano Shrine|Q3472357|qid=Q3472357|1=Sano Shrine}} **Higashi Kirishima Shrine **Kirishima Higashi Shrine **{{ill|lt=Kirishima Jingū|Q704622|qid=Q704622|1=Kirishima Jingū}} * {{ill|lt=Hoko-in (Kobayashi)|Q17219459|qid=Q17219459|lt=Hoko-in|1=Hoko-in}} - former betto temple before Hinamori Gongen was merged into Kirishimamine Shrine {{神道 横}} === External links === * [https://minezinzya.wixsite.com/kirishimamine Official website of Kirishimamine Shrine] {{shinto-stub}} {{pref-stub|pref=宮崎県}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Kirishimamine Shrine]][[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hyūga Province]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Hyūga Province]] [[Category:山岳信仰]] [[Category:Former Prefectural Shrines in Miyazaki Prefecture]] [[Category:小林市の建築物]] [[Category:小林市の歴史]]
